subject: Mathematics

unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Unit 5! In this unit you will learn how to calculate with numbers up to 100. Calculating means working things out with numbers – adding to find how many there are altogether, subtracting to find how many are left, multiplying when you have equal groups, and dividing when you share things out fairly. You already do these things every day. When Amina adds the goats her family keeps to the goats her uncle keeps, she is adding. When Yusuf gives some of his dates to his little sister and counts what is left, he is subtracting. When Halima lays out 4 rows of 5 sambusas on a tray, she is really multiplying. When Khadija shares 12 mangoes equally between 3 friends, she is dividing.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, activities and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured from the Cambridge Primary Mathematics 0096 — Stage 2 workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: This unit is designed for self-paced learning. That means you move at your own speed. Read each explanation slowly, try every worked example yourself on paper, and only move on when you feel ready. Your AI tutor is available day and night to give you extra practice, explain a step in a new way, or check your answers. Your teacher will also meet with you about twice a week to help with anything that feels tricky.

Why does this matter so much? We calculate every single day. Traders in the Hargeisa market add up prices and work out the right change. Farmers subtract to know how many goats are still out grazing. A cook multiplies to know how many dates are needed if every child at iftar gets three. Families divide food fairly so everyone has an equal share, just as we are taught to share in charity and zakat. When you become confident with these four operations, you gain a skill you will use in shops, at home, at the masjid and in every future maths lesson.

As you work, keep a pencil and some paper beside you. Mathematics is not a subject you can learn only by reading – you must do it. Draw the number lines, build the arrays with stones or beans, and say the answers out loud. If you get something wrong, that is not a failure; it is simply a sign of exactly what to practise next. Every mathematician you admire once sat where you sit now, learning these very same ideas.
Add ones and tens to two-digit numbers using clear strategies.
Subtract ones and tens from two-digit numbers.
Add three or more single-digit numbers efficiently by looking for pairs that make ten.
Understand multiplication as repeated addition and as arrays.
Build and recall the 1, 2, 5 and 10 times tables.
Understand division as repeated subtraction, grouping and sharing.

concepts.title: Adding Ones to Two-Digit Numbers
concepts.explanation: Addition helps us find the total when we combine groups. When we add a one-digit number to a two-digit number, there are several strategies, and a good mathematician chooses the one that fits the numbers best. Let us learn three of them using the example 42 + 6. Strategy 1 – The place value grid. A place value grid keeps the tens and the ones in their own columns so nothing gets muddled. Write 42 as 4 tens and 2 ones. The 6 is 6 ones, so it goes in the ones column. Now add the ones: 2 ones + 6 ones = 8 ones. The…
concepts.example: Strategy 2 – Number line jumps. Start at the larger number, 42, and jump forward by ones: 43, 44, 45, 46, 47, 48. That is six little jumps of one, and we land on 48. Picture Farah hopping along a row of stepping stones,…

concepts.title: Adding Tens to Two-Digit Numbers
concepts.explanation: Adding tens is wonderfully easy once you spot the pattern. When we add whole tens to a two-digit number, the ones digit stays exactly the same and only the tens digit grows. Look at 42 + 10 = 52. The ones digit is still 2, but the tens digit has gone from 4 to 5. Nothing else moved. Here are more examples. Read each one and check the ones digit never changes:

concepts.title: Finding Complements of 10, 20 and 100
concepts.explanation: A complement is the number you need to add to reach a target. If the target is 10 and you already have 7, the complement is 3, because 7 + 3 = 10. Knowing complements by heart makes adding and subtracting lightning fast, so these are facts worth learning until you never have to think about them. Complements of 10 are the pairs that make ten:
concepts.example: Complements of 20 work the same way, just with a bigger target. For example 12 + 8 = 20, 11 + 9 = 20, and 10 + 10 = 20. Here is a lovely shortcut: if you already know that 6 + 4 = 10, then you also know that 16 + 4 =…

concepts.title: Adding Three or More Numbers
concepts.explanation: When you add three or more small numbers, you do not have to add them in the order they are written. Addition can be done in any order, so the clever thing is to hunt for a pair that makes ten first. Ten is an easy, friendly number to add on to, so making a ten early makes the rest simple. Take 4 + 7 + 6. Scan the numbers and look for a pair that makes ten. Here 4 + 6 = 10. Add those first, then add the number left over: 10 + 7 = 17. So 4 + 7 + 6 = 17. We rearranged the numbers to make our life easier, and the…
concepts.example: Another example: 5 + 8 + 5. The pair 5 + 5 = 10 jumps out. Add those first, then add the 8: 10 + 8 = 18. So 5 + 8 + 5 = 18. Sometimes there is more than one pair. In 6 + 5 + 9 + 5 you could pair 6 with... well, 6 + 4 is…

concepts.title: Subtracting Ones from Two-Digit Numbers
concepts.explanation: Subtraction helps us find what is left when we take away, and it also tells us the difference between two numbers. Just like addition, there are several strategies for taking a one-digit number away from a two-digit number. Let us use 47 − 5. Strategy 1 – The place value grid. Write 47 as 4 tens and 7 ones. We are taking away 5 ones. Subtract in the ones column: 7 ones − 5 ones = 2 ones. The tens stay as 4 tens. So the answer is 4 tens and 2 ones, which is 42. So 47 − 5 = 42. Because there were enough ones to take…
concepts.example: Strategy 2 – Number line jumps. Start at the larger number, 47, and jump backward by ones: 46, 45, 44, 43, 42. That is five jumps back, landing on 42. Subtraction moves us to the left on the number line, the opposite…

concepts.title: Subtracting Tens from Two-Digit Numbers
concepts.explanation: Subtracting whole tens follows the same friendly pattern as adding them, only backwards. When we subtract tens, the ones digit stays the same and only the tens digit gets smaller. Look at 53 − 10 = 43. The ones digit is still 3, but the tens digit has dropped from 5 to 4. 47 − 10 = 37 (ones stays 7, tens goes 4 → 3)
concepts.example: 85 − 20 = 65 (ones stays 5, tens goes 8 → 6, because 20 is two tens)

methods.title: How to add a one-digit number by making a ten
steps: Start at the larger number.
Work out how many more you need to reach the next ten.
Split the small number and add that part first to reach the ten.
Add whatever is left.

methods.title: How to choose the operation in a word problem
steps: Combining groups or finding a total? Add.
Taking away, or finding how many are left or the difference? Subtract.
Equal groups and you want the total? Multiply.
Know the total and want equal parts? Divide.

methods.title: How to check a calculation
steps: Check addition by adding again, perhaps in a different order.
Check subtraction by adding your answer back on.
Check division by multiplying your answer.

methods.title: Add three numbers cleverly
steps: Solution: Look for a pair that makes ten.
Add those first, then add the 7: 10 + 7 = 17.

methods.title: Subtract and check
methods.example: Work out 62 − 9, then check your answer.
steps: Solution: Use number bonds: 12 − 9 = 3, and there are 5 tens carried along, so 62 − 9 = 53.
Check by adding back: 53 + 9 = 62.
It matches, so the answer is correct.

methods.title: Choose add or subtract
methods.example: There are 45 children in the playground. 12 more children join them. How many children are there now?
steps: Solution: More children *join*, so we are combining groups – that means add.
45 + 12 = 57 children.
Check the choose add or subtract result carefully.

workedExamples.title: Add ones by making a ten
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Start at 56. To reach the next ten, 60, we need 4. Split the 7 into 4 and 3. Add the 4 first: 56 + 4 = 60. Then add the remaining 3: 60 + 3 = 63. So 56 + 7 = 63.

workedExamples.title: Add tens
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Adding tens leaves the ones alone. The ones digit stays 8. Add the tens: 3 tens + 3 tens = 6 tens. So the answer is 6 tens and 8 ones, which is 68. So 38 + 30 = 68.

workedExamples.title: Find a complement
workedExamples.solution: Solution: We need the complement of 30 to 100. Think in tens: 3 tens plus how many tens make 10 tens? 3 + 7 = 10, so 30 + 70 = 100. The missing number is 70.

workedExamples.title: Add three numbers cleverly
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Look for a pair that makes ten. Here 6 + 4 = 10. Add those first, then add the 7: 10 + 7 = 17. So 6 + 7 + 4 = 17.

workedExamples.title: Subtract and check
workedExamples.prompt: Work out 62 − 9, then check your answer.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Use number bonds: 12 − 9 = 3, and there are 5 tens carried along, so 62 − 9 = 53. Check by adding back: 53 + 9 = 62. It matches, so the answer is correct.

workedExamples.title: Choose add or subtract
workedExamples.prompt: There are 45 children in the playground. 12 more children join them. How many children are there now?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: More children *join*, so we are combining groups – that means add. 45 + 12 = 57 children.

workedExamples.title: Multiplication as repeated addition
workedExamples.prompt: Write 5 + 5 + 5 + 5 as a multiplication and find the total.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: There are 4 groups of 5, so it is 4 × 5. Counting in fives: 5, 10, 15, 20. So 5 + 5 + 5 + 5 = 4 × 5 = 20.

workedExamples.title: Division by sharing
workedExamples.prompt: There are 40 cupcakes shared equally among 8 children. How many cupcakes does each child get?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: We know the total (40) and the number of groups (8), and we want how many each, so we divide: 40 ÷ 8 = 5 cupcakes each. Check with multiplication: 8 × 5 = 40. Correct.

workedExamples.title: Add three numbers by finding a ten
workedExamples.prompt: Add three numbers by finding a ten: a) 4 + 8 + 6 b) 7 + 3 + 5 c) 8 + 5 + 5.

workedExamples.title: Complete the times tables
workedExamples.prompt: Complete the times tables: a) 2 × 8 b) 5 × 6 c) 10 × 4.

workedExamples.title: Write each as a multiplication
workedExamples.prompt: Write each as a multiplication: a) 5 + 5 + 5 b) 10 + 10 + 10.

workedExamples.title: Division as grouping
workedExamples.prompt: Division as grouping: a) 15 ÷ 5 b) 14 ÷ 2 c) 40 ÷ 10.

activities.title: Number Line Jumps
activities.materials: a long strip of paper, a pencil, a small stone as a counter.
steps: Draw a number line from 0 to 20 and mark every number.
Place your stone on a starting number, for example 8.
To add, hop the stone forward; to subtract, hop it backward. Say each jump out loud.
Ask a family member to call out sums like ‘12 + 5’ and ‘17 − 6’ and hop to find the answer.

activities.title: Complement Pairs Snap
activities.materials: small cards with the numbers 0 to 10 written on them.
steps: Shuffle the cards and place them face down between two players.
Take turns turning over two cards.
If the two cards make a complement of 10 (like 7 and 3), shout ‘Ten!’ and keep the pair.
The player with the most pairs when the cards run out wins.
This game trains your bonds to 10 until they become automatic.

activities.title: Build an Array with Stones
activities.materials: a handful of stones, beans or bottle caps.
steps: Lay out 3 rows with 4 stones in each row.
Count the stones in total. Write the multiplication: 3 × 4 = 12.
Now turn the array so it is 4 rows of 3. Count again – it is still 12!
Build two more arrays of your own and write the multiplication for each.

activities.title: Fair Sharing at Home (Division)
activities.materials: a bowl of dates, sweets or stones, and some plates or cups.
steps: Count out 12 dates and set out 3 plates.
Deal the dates onto the plates one at a time, going round and round, until they are gone.
Count how many are on each plate. Write it: 12 ÷ 3 = 4.
Try again with 20 dates on 4 plates, and 15 dates on 5 plates.

activities.title: Counting Stick Times-Table Investigation
activities.materials: a stick or a ruler, and a partner.
steps: Imagine the stick is divided into ten equal parts.
Touch each part and count in twos: 2, 4, 6, 8, 10, and on to 20.
Do it again counting in fives, then in tens.
Ask your partner to point to a part and say the multiplication fact, for example the 4th part in fives is 5 × 4 = 20.

activities.title: The Market Stall (Family Game)
activities.materials: small items as ‘goods’, stones as ‘shillings’, and your family.
steps: Set up a pretend market stall with prices under 100 shillings.
One person is the shopkeeper and one is the customer.
The customer buys two or three items – add the prices to find the total.
Pay with more shillings than needed, then subtract to work out the correct change. Swap roles and play again.

After your activities, talk or write about: Which activity helped you understand the four operations best? What was the trickiest part? Did any game make a times table or number bond easier to remember? Share your answer with your teacher at your next session.

rules.title: Key rule 1
rules.text: Adding and Subtracting Tens When you add or subtract whole tens, the ones digit stays the same. 42 + 10 = 52. 53 − 10 = 43. Only the tens digit changes.

rules.title: Complement Rule
rules.text: A complement is the missing part that completes the whole. Bonds to 10: 1+9, 2+8, 3+7, 4+6, 5+5. 3 + 7 = 10, so 30 + 70 = 100. Bonds to 10 unlock bonds to 100.

rules.title: Key rule 3
rules.text: Multiplication and Division are Opposites Multiplication is repeated addition of equal groups: 3 × 5 = 15. Division shares or groups: 15 ÷ 5 = 3 and 15 ÷ 3 = 5. One undoes the other, so check a division by multiplying.

terms: Put groups together to find how many altogether
Take away, or find how many more
The missing part that completes a target
Equal rows and columns of objects
Share equally or make equal groups
Two ways to think about division
commonMistakes: Changing the ones when adding tens
Find a pair that makes 10 first, then add the rest
Add the answer back to see if it returns the start

Use 2 × 7 = 14 to write two division facts.
Check these and correct any that are wrong: a) 47 − 5 = 42 b) 63 + 20 = 73 c) 58 − 30 = 18.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: a) correct (42), b) incorrect, should be 83, c) incorrect, should be 28.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain the key idea in Subtracting Ones from Two-Digit Numbers.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Subtraction helps us find what is left when we take away, and it also tells us the difference between two numbers. Just like addition, there are several strategies for taking a one-digit number away from a two-digit number. Let us use 47 − 5. Strategy 1 – The place value grid. Write 47 as 4 tens and 7 ones. We are taking away 5 ones. Subtract in the ones column: 7 ones − 5 ones = 2 ones. The tens stay as 4 tens. So the answer is 4 tens and 2 ones, which is 42. So 47 − 5 = 42. Because there were enough ones to take…

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain the key idea in Subtracting Tens from Two-Digit Numbers.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Subtracting whole tens follows the same friendly pattern as adding them, only backwards. When we subtract tens, the ones digit stays the same and only the tens digit gets smaller. Look at 53 − 10 = 43. The ones digit is still 3, but the tens digit has dropped from 5 to 4. 47 − 10 = 37 (ones stays 7, tens goes 4 → 3)

questions.question: Work out 56 + 7.
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, using the Unit 5 methods.
questions.explanation: Start at 56. To reach the next ten, 60, we need 4. Split the 7 into 4 and 3. Add the 4 first: 56 + 4 = 60. Then add the remaining 3: 60 + 3 = 63. So 56 + 7 = 63.

questions.question: Work out 38 + 30.
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, using the Unit 5 methods.
questions.explanation: Adding tens leaves the ones alone. The ones digit stays 8. Add the tens: 3 tens + 3 tens = 6 tens. So the answer is 6 tens and 8 ones, which is 68. So 38 + 30 = 68.

questions.question: Work out 6 + 7 + 4.
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, using the Unit 5 methods.
questions.explanation: Look for a pair that makes ten. Here 6 + 4 = 10. Add those first, then add the 7: 10 + 7 = 17. So 6 + 7 + 4 = 17.

questions.question: Work out 62 − 9, then check your answer.
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, using the Unit 5 methods.
questions.explanation: Use number bonds: 12 − 9 = 3, and there are 5 tens carried along, so 62 − 9 = 53. Check by adding back: 53 + 9 = 62. It matches, so the answer is correct.

questions.question: Write 5 + 5 + 5 + 5 as a multiplication and find the total.
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, using the Unit 5 methods.
questions.explanation: There are 4 groups of 5, so it is 4 × 5. Counting in fives: 5, 10, 15, 20. So 5 + 5 + 5 + 5 = 4 × 5 = 20.

questions.question: In the number 63, what is the value of the digit 3?
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, then check each option.
questions.explanation: 3 is in the ones place, so it is worth 3.

questions.question: Which number is greater: 58 or 61?
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, then check each option.

questions.question: In the number 62, what is the value of the digit 6?
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, then check each option.
questions.explanation: 6 is in the tens place, so it is worth 60.

questions.question: What number comes just after 65?
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, then check each option.

questions.question: What does “Add / Total” mean?

Take away, or find how many more
The missing part that completes a target

games.title: Concept Quest: Subtract / Difference
games.description: Practise subtract / difference through four short challenges.

rounds.clue: Work it out step by step, using the Unit 5 methods.
rounds.explanation: Adding tens leaves the ones alone. The ones digit stays 8. Add the tens: 3 tens + 3 tens = 6 tens. So the answer is 6 tens and 8 ones, which is 68. So 38 + 30 = 68.

rounds.prompt: Write 5 + 5 + 5 + 5 as a multiplication and find the total.
rounds.clue: Work it out step by step, using the Unit 5 methods.
rounds.explanation: There are 4 groups of 5, so it is 4 × 5. Counting in fives: 5, 10, 15, 20. So 5 + 5 + 5 + 5 = 4 × 5 = 20.

rounds.prompt: In the number 62, what is the value of the digit 6?
rounds.clue: Work it out step by step, then check each option.
rounds.explanation: 6 is in the tens place, so it is worth 60.

rounds.prompt: Work out 62 − 9, then check your answer.
rounds.clue: Work it out step by step, using the Unit 5 methods.
rounds.explanation: Use number bonds: 12 − 9 = 3, and there are 5 tens carried along, so 62 − 9 = 53. Check by adding back: 53 + 9 = 62. It matches, so the answer is correct.

selfAssessment: I can this unit is designed for self-paced learning. That means you move at your own speed. Read each explanation slowly, try every worked example yourself on paper, and only move on when you feel ready. Your AI tutor is available day and night to give you extra practice, explain a step in a new way, or check your answers. Your teacher will also meet with you about twice a week to help with anything that feels tricky.

I can why does this matter so much? We calculate every single day. Traders in the Hargeisa market add up prices and work out the right change. Farmers subtract to know how many goats are still out grazing. A cook multiplies to know how many dates are needed if every child at iftar gets three. Families divide food fairly so everyone has an equal share, just as we are taught to share in charity and zakat. When you become confident with these four operations, you gain a skill you will use in shops, at home, at the masjid and in every future maths lesson.

I can as you work, keep a pencil and some paper beside you. Mathematics is not a subject you can learn only by reading – you must do it. Draw the number lines, build the arrays with stones or beans, and say the answers out loud. If you get something wrong, that is not a failure; it is simply a sign of exactly what to practise next. Every mathematician you admire once sat where you sit now, learning these very same ideas.
I can add ones and tens to two-digit numbers using clear strategies.
I can subtract ones and tens from two-digit numbers.
I can find complements of 10, 20 and 100.
I can add three or more single-digit numbers efficiently by looking for pairs that make ten.
I can understand multiplication as repeated addition and as arrays.
